title: How I Use Claude to Build My Entire Business
summary: From writing code to drafting proposals to planning automations — how Claude AI became the core tool behind everything I build at Solvr Labs.
context: Jessy uses Claude Code for building the website, generating blog posts, writing automation logic, drafting client proposals, and even planning the architecture for TaskLine. This post covers the day-to-day reality of building a one-person AI company with AI as your co-builder.

title: I Automated This Blog With AI and GitHub Actions
summary: How I set up a system that generates a new blog post every week using Claude API, GitHub Actions, and zero manual work.
context: Meta post about the blog automation itself. Covers the topics queue, the generation script, the GitHub Action cron schedule, and how Vercel auto-deploys. Honest about what works and what the limitations are. Talks about how the topics come from real client conversations and build sessions.

title: n8n vs Zapier: Why I Switched and Never Looked Back
summary: Zapier is easy. n8n is powerful. Here's why I use n8n for every client automation and when Zapier still makes sense.
context: Compare the two from a builder's perspective. n8n is self-hostable, has no per-task pricing, supports complex branching and code nodes. Zapier is simpler but gets expensive fast and has limited logic. For service businesses doing 500+ automations/month, n8n saves thousands. Include real examples from client work.

title: What AI Can't Do for Your Business (Yet)
summary: AI is powerful but it's not magic. Here's where it falls short and what still needs a human touch.
context: Honest post about limitations. AI can't replace relationship-building, on-site judgment calls, or handle truly novel situations. It hallucinates. It needs guardrails. It's bad at knowing what it doesn't know. But for repeatable, structured tasks — it's already better than most humans. The goal is human + AI, not AI replacing humans.

title: How a Plumber Saves 10 Hours a Week With AI
summary: A real breakdown of the automations I built for a plumbing company — missed call handling, proposal generation, and invoice follow-ups.
context: Composite case study based on real client patterns. Walk through each automation: missed call → AI text-back → booking link → calendar event → Discord notification. Proposal flow: job details → Claude drafts proposal → owner reviews → sends via SMS. Invoice flow: job marked complete → Stripe invoice generated → sent → auto follow-up at 3, 7, 14 days. Include rough time savings and cost numbers.

title: Building in Public: Why I Share Everything
summary: Most agencies hide their process. I share mine. Here's why transparency is my best marketing strategy.
context: Post about the philosophy behind the blog and building in public. As a solo founder, trust is everything. Sharing the actual tools, code approaches, and real results builds credibility faster than any sales page. Talk about how the blog itself is proof of the systems — it's automated, AI-generated from real work, and ships without manual intervention.

title: Voice AI in 2026: What's Actually Good Now
summary: Voice AI has improved dramatically. Here's what's production-ready, what's still rough, and what I'm using for client projects.
context: Cover the current state of voice AI platforms. Latency has dropped to near-real-time. Voices sound natural. But interruption handling is still tricky, multilingual support varies, and some platforms charge per minute which adds up. Compare from a builder's perspective — what matters for service business phone systems specifically.

title: Supabase for Everything: Why It's My Go-To Backend
summary: PostgreSQL, auth, real-time, storage, edge functions — all in one platform. Here's how I use Supabase for client projects and TaskLine.
context: Technical but accessible post about why Supabase is the best backend for solo developers and small teams. Row-level security for multi-tenant apps, real-time subscriptions for live dashboards, edge functions for webhooks. Compare to Firebase and raw Postgres setups. Include specific patterns from TaskLine like the proposal storage and feedback loop.
